rulings.Case Title: WILLI SCHNEIDER ET AL VS. HONEYWELL INTERNATIONAL INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, March 8, 2017 in Department 503, Line 1. Defendant Frederick Meiswinkel, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication of plaintiffs' Negligence II and Loss of Consortium causes of action are denied. Defendant sustained its initial burden pursuant to the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. The deposition testimony and declaration of Willi Schneider and the declaration of Charles Ay create a triable issue whether Mr. Schneider was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials (fireproofing) attributable to defendant. If a hearing is requested, it will be at 9:30a.m. A court reporter will not be provided by the court. If any party wishes to have the matter reported, the parties must meet and confer to agree on only one court reporter for any reported matter in this case. There will be only one official record in a case. The designated reporter must be licensed pursuant to Bus. & Prof. Code, Sec. 8016. If the parties are unable to agree on one reporter, the court will designate a qualified court reporter that will provide the official transcript for the matter. The party or parties shall pay for the cost of reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:30pm stating their intention to contest. If a tentative ruling is contested, the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing. If a tentative ruling is not contested, the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must either bring the proposed order to the hearing or submit it after the hearing in compliance with CRC 3.1312. = (503/CML)

rulings.Case Title: LARRY WALKER VS. CRANE CO. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT By Defendant George Paul Baba Sued Herein As Doe 301.
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Wednesday, March 8, 2017 in Department 503, Line 2. Defendant George Paul Baba's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff's claim is precluded by the Workers' Compensation exclusivity rule, as defendant concedes that plaintiff was not employed by defendant during the time(s) at issue. Defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff's claim is precluded by the doctrine set forth in Privette v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 689 and its progeny. Assuming that defendant did not retain control over plaintiff and/or plaintiff's work and that there was no concealed preexisting hazardous condition on the property, defendant still cannot demonstrate that plaintiff's Negligence II cause of action must fail. Plaintiff's claims are based on the premise that the actions of defendant's employees affirmatively contributed to plaintiff's exposure to asbestos. (See Biles v. Exxon Mobil Corp. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1315.) Notwithstanding this conclusion, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden on its Privette argument under either prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar because it failed to demonstrate that plaintiff failed to provide meaningful responses to comprehensive interrogatories designed to elicit all the evidence plaintiff has to support his contention of liability. Defendant also failed to submit evidence sufficient to negate the duty element of plaintiff's cause of action. Mr. Baba's declaration is not sufficient to do so. Assuming defendant properly raised the issue of threshold exposure/causation, defendant failed to sustain its initial burden on this issue. Defendant is precluded from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar because it failed to demonstrate that plaintiff failed to provide meaningful responses to comprehensive interrogatories designed to elicit all the evidence plaintiff has to support his contention of liability. Defendant also failed to submit evidence sufficient to negate the causation element of plaintiff's cause of action. Mr. Baba's declaration is not sufficient to do so. At most, Mr. Baba's declaration, when read in conjunction with plaintiff's deposition testimony, creates a triable issue of material fact whether the workers/laborers at issue were employed by defendant. Plaintiff's objections to the declaration of George Baba: Objection no. 1 is sustained as to "There was no asbestos on the construction site," and overruled as to "and I was not aware of any asbestos being used." Objections no. 2 and no. 3 are overruled. Objection no. 4 is sustained. The Court did not consider defendant's reply evidence. (Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243; San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308.) (Tentative Ruling continued.)
